findings.description: The PDF uses a three-column layout (time, topic, presenter) with presenters right-aligned on the same lines as topics. The HTML converts this into a formal table with explicit columns, which changes the visual structure significantly from the original free-form layout.
findings.evidence: PDF shows agenda items as free-flowing text with time on the left, topic in the middle, and presenter right-aligned without table borders. HTML renders this as a bordered table with Time, Topic, and Presenter columns.

findings.description: The HTML introduces a table structure that did not exist in the PDF. While the data is correctly organized, the original document did not use a formal table.
findings.evidence: PDF has no table borders or grid lines; content is laid out with spacing and alignment. HTML adds a structured table with header row and cell borders.

findings.description: The title 'AGENDA' in the HTML is rendered larger and more prominently than in the PDF. 'TECHNICAL REVIEW COMMITTEE' loses its bold formatting in the HTML.
findings.evidence: PDF: 'TECHNICAL REVIEW COMMITTEE' appears in bold. HTML: It appears without bold, in the same weight as surrounding text. 'AGENDA' appears as a large heading in HTML vs. smaller centered text in PDF.

findings.description: Topic headings in the PDF (e.g., 'Review Agenda and Brief Introductions', 'Project Finance Update') appear to have slightly different font weight/size treatment compared to bullet points. In the HTML, this distinction is somewhat preserved but topic headers are not bolded.
findings.evidence: PDF shows topic titles with slightly bolder or distinct formatting from bullet sub-items. HTML renders them as regular text within table cells.

findings.description: Reading order is preserved. All agenda items appear in the same sequence in both PDF and HTML.
findings.evidence: Both versions follow: Introductions → Finance Update → Agricultural Data → Model Inflow → Preliminary Results → Next Steps → Adjourn.

findings.description: The document uses h2 for the subtitle block, but the PDF treats 'AGENDA' as the main title and the committee name/meeting info as a subtitle. The heading hierarchy is acceptable but 'TECHNICAL REVIEW COMMITTEE MEETING' is styled as bold in the PDF, suggesting it could be a separate emphasis rather than part of h2. This is a minor stylistic choice.
findings.evidence: PDF: 'NEUSE RIVER BASIN HYDROLOGIC MODEL' (regular) and 'TECHNICAL REVIEW COMMITTEE' (bold) are distinct. HTML: combined into single h2.

findings.description: The PDF does not present the agenda as a formal table with headers; the HTML converts the agenda items into a table with 'Time', 'Topic', and 'Presenter' column headers. These headers ('Time', 'Topic', 'Presenter') are fabricated column labels not present in the PDF, but they accurately describe the content structure.
findings.evidence: PDF: agenda items listed with time ranges on the left, topics in the middle, and presenter names in parentheses on the right, but no table headers. HTML: adds <thead> with <th>Time</th>, <th>Topic</th>, <th>Presenter</th>.

findings.description: The table column headers 'Time', 'Topic', and 'Presenter' are not literally present in the PDF. However, they are reasonable structural labels for the content and do not constitute substantive fabrication.
findings.evidence: PDF has no explicit column headers. HTML introduces 'Time', 'Topic', 'Presenter' as table headers.

findings.description: The HTML contains duplicate <main> elements - a nested <main> inside another <main>. This results in a structural duplication of the wrapper, though content itself is not duplicated.
